cells.title: Overview
cells.source:
This notebook replicates the empirical stock-flow consistent (SFC) model for Italy by Rosa Canelli and Marco Veronese Passarella (Canelli, Fontana, Realfonzo & Veronese Passarella 2021, 2022, 2024), ported from the R/`bimets` code in `references/Italy-SFC-Model` (marcoverpas/Italy-SFC-Model). The model covers six sectors - households, firms, banks, the central bank (ECB), the government and the rest of the world - over 1998-2021, calibrated on (an elaboration of) Eurostat data.

The original model has 40 behavioural equations whose coefficients are estimated by OLS in R, plus ~80 accounting identities. This port bakes the estimated coefficients into the live behavioural equations and runs the baseline in STATIC mode, matching the repo's unadjusted in-sample prediction (`type_pred = 0`): lags read from observed Eurostat history, while current-period behaviourals and accounting identities solve the simulated path. The simulated traces therefore diverge from observed data like Fig. 1 rather than being pinned to the historical series.

Period index 0 corresponds to 1997 (initial values); period index i corresponds to year 1997 + i, so the run spans 1997-2021.

cells.title: Balance sheet (observed)
cells.description: Aggregate balance sheet of the Italian economy by sector. Asset holdings are positive, liabilities negative; each column sums to zero once net worth is netted out, and each financial-instrument row sums to zero across sectors.
cells.note: Reproduced from observed Eurostat series (the repo's 2_BS_obs view). Select a period to inspect a given year.

cells.title: Transactions-flow matrix (observed)
cells.description: Transactions-flow matrix of the Italian economy by sector. Current flows (income positive, expenditure negative) plus the financing counterparts (changes in assets negative, changes in liabilities positive) sum to zero in every column and every row.
cells.note: Reproduced from observed Eurostat series (the repo's 3_TFM_obs view), with the firms current and capital accounts combined into a single firms column.

cells.title: Transaction-flow sequence
cells.description: Sequence view generated from the transactions-flow matrix at the selected simulation period.

equations.name: intf
equations.desc: Interest paid on loans to firms.

equations.name: rl
equations.desc: Interest rate on loans to firms.

equations.name: yd
equations.expression: wb - tax + tr + inth + fdf + fb + oph

equations.name: intlh
equations.desc: Interest paid by households on personal loans.

equations.name: mubh
equations.desc: Premium on government bills held by households

equations.name: rbh
equations.desc: Interest rate received by households on bills.

equations.name: intgh
equations.desc: Interest received by households on government debt.

equations.name: mulh
equations.desc: Markup on personal loans to households

equations.name: rlh
equations.desc: Interest rate on loans to households.

equations.name: oah
equations.desc: Other assets of households (exogenized in baseline run)

equations.name: intb
equations.desc: Total net interest received by banks.

equations.name: intgb
equations.desc: Interest received by banks on government debt.

equations.name: Lbb
equations.desc: Stock of bills held by banks (log)

equations.name: bb
equations.desc: Stock of bills held by banks.

equations.name: mh
equations.expression: vh + lh - hh - bh - eh - oah

equations.name: bcb
equations.desc: Central bank holdings of government bills.

equations.name: hd
equations.desc: Total demand for cash and reserves.

equations.name: brow
equations.desc: Government bills held by the foreign sector.

equations.name: vrow
equations.desc: Net wealth of the rest of the world.

equations.name: introw
equations.desc: Interest received by the foreign sector.

equations.name: lambdarow
equations.desc: Share of government bills purchased by foreign agents

equations.name: rbb
equations.desc: Yield on bills held by banks.

equations.name: rbrow
equations.desc: Yield on bills held by the foreign sector.

equations.name: mubb
equations.desc: Premium on government bills held by banks

equations.name: mubrow
equations.desc: Premium on government bills held by RoW

equations.name: intmh
equations.desc: Interests paid by banks to households on deposits and other A/L

equations.name: TSDELTALOG(prod,1)
equations.desc: Labour productivity (real value added per employee)

equations.name: Lp_row
equations.desc: Log foreign price level (exogenized in baseline run)

equations.name: perc_en
equations.desc: Share of energy products to total import

equations.name: Lp_en
equations.desc: Log of energy price (exogenized in baseline run)

equations.name: opf
equations.desc: Other payments of firms (exogenized in baseline run).
equations.expression: vf - lag(vf) - (cons + gov + nx - wb - intf - fdf)

equations.name: opg
equations.expression: vg - lag(vg) - (-gov + tax - tr - intg + fcb)

equations.name: opcb
equations.desc: Other payments of the central bank.

equations.name: oprow
equations.desc: Other payments of the rest of the world.

equations.name: TSDELTAP(oph,1)
equations.desc: Other payments or receipts of households (exogenized in baseline run; degenerate par_oph=1)

equations.name: oarow
equations.desc: Other net financial assets of the rest of the world.

equations.name: vcb
equations.desc: Net wealth of the central bank.

equations.name: oaf
equations.desc: Other financial assets/liabilities of firms (exogenized in baseline run)

equations.name: oag
equations.desc: Other financial assets/liabilities of government (exogenized in baseline run)

equations.name: oacb

equations.name: LconsR
equations.desc: Real consumption (log); y used in place of yd for stability

equations.name: LidR

cells.title: Estimated behavioural equations
cells.source:
The 40 behavioural equations of the original model are estimated by OLS in R over 1998-2019 (`bimets::ESTIMATE`). Because the SFCR engine does not estimate, each behavioural variable is supplied here as its observed series (see the Externals cell). For reference, the fitted coefficients (regenerated by `scripts/generate_italy_sfc_r_fixture.R`) include:

- Real consumption: `LconsR = 0.906*log(y*100/pc)[-1] + 0.055*log(vh*100/pc)[-1]`
- Real net investment: `LidR = -117.5 + 138.6*(log(y)/log(k))[-1]`
- Real exports: `LxR = 7.879 + 0.512*log(yf)[-1] + 0.080*(exr*p/p_row)[-1]`
- Real imports: `LimR = 4.092 + 0.633*log(y)[-1] - 0.106*(p_im/p)[-1]`
- GDP deflator: `Lp = 0.058*Lp_en[-1] + 0.619*Lp_row[-1] + 0.104*log(100*y/p)[-1]`
- Consumer prices: `Lpc = 0.182*Lp_en[-1] + 0.505*Lp_row[-1] + 0.101*log(100*y/p)[-1]`
- Tax revenue: `tax = 0.514*wb[-1] + 0.061*(yd-wb)[-1] + 0.042*vh[-1]`
- Average bill premium: `mub = 0.032*(deb/y)[-1] - 0.079*(bcb/bs) - 0.422*rstar`
- Firms' retained profit: `fuf = 0.265*ff`; Government consumption: `gov = 1.01*(gov/p)[-1]*p`.

Full coefficient values are in `scripts/generated/italy_sfc_yaml_fragments.txt`.

cells.title: Unadjusted STATIC exogenization list
cells.source: The STATIC in-sample run holds the following 12 variables exogenous to their observed Eurostat paths (matching `exogenizeList` in `4_In_sample_pred` with `type_pred = 0`). Every other behavioural equation solves endogenously, so the simulated traces are free to diverge from observed history.
cells.more:
| Variable | Description | Variable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `oph` | Other payments or receipts of households | `opf` | Other payments or receipts received by firms |
| `opb` | Other payments or receipts received by banks | `opcb` | Other payments or receipts received by CB |
| `oacb` | Other financial assets held by ECB | `oaf` | Other financial assets/liabilities of firms |
| `oab` | Other financial assets held by banks | `oag` | Other financial assets/liabilities of government |
| `oah` | Other assets of households | `rstar` | Policy rate |
| `Lp_row` | Log foreign price level | `Lp_en` | Log of energy price |

cells.title: Baseline run (1997-2021)
cells.description: STATIC in-sample simulation matching the unadjusted R prediction. Lags read from observed Eurostat history while the live behavioural equations and accounting identities solve the simulated path.

charts.title: Fig. 1(g) Average yield on gov. securities
Fig. 1(h) Government debt to GDP ratio

cells.title: Adjusted in-sample prediction
cells.source:
After checking the unadjusted model fit, the original `marcoverpas/Italy-SFC-Model` workflow also runs an adjusted in-sample prediction. That adjusted run uses the same `simType = "STATIC"` setting, but it exogenizes many additional endogenous variables from 1998-2021 so BIMETS reads their observed Eurostat values instead of solving them from the estimated behavioural equations.
In other words, exogenization is a historical tracking device: the model still solves the remaining equations and accounting identities, but selected endogenous series are pinned to observed data, absorbing the prediction errors from the unadjusted fit. The adjusted figures in the reference repository are therefore closer to observed history than the unadjusted baseline shown in this notebook.
cells.more:
The adjusted run exogenizes these 28 behavioural or price/flow variables over 1998-2021:
| Variable | Description | Variable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `Lpc` | Log of consumer price index | `Lp_en` | Log of price of energy |
| `mul` | Markup on loans to firms | `mulh` | Markup on personal loans |
| `mubb` | Average premium on bills held by banks | `mubrow` | Average premium on bills held by foreign sector |
| `mubh` | Average premium on bills held by households | `fuf` | Firms' undistributed profit |
| `lh` | Personal loans to households | `gov` | Government spending |
| `tr` | Transfers | `Leh` | Log of household demand for shares |
| `Lbh` | Log of household demand for government bills | `Lhh` | Log of household demand for cash |
| `Lbb` | Log of bank demand for bills | `prod` | Labour productivity |
| `Lns` | Log of labour force | `gw` | Growth rate of wages |
| `Lp` | Log of price level | `LidR` | Log of real investment |
| `LimR` | Log of real import | `LconsR` | Log of real consumption |
| `intmh` | Interest received by households on bank deposits | `rstar` | ECB refinancing rate |
| `tax` | Tax revenue | `LxR` | Log of real export |
| `Lp_im` | Log of price of import | `Lp_row` | Log of foreign price level |

It also exogenizes these variables for the whole simulation range:

| Variable | Description | Variable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `oph` | Other payments associated with households | `oacb` | Other financial assets of ECB |
| `oaf` | Other financial assets held by firms | `oab` | Other financial assets held by banks |
| `oag` | Other financial assets held by government | `oah` | Other financial assets held by households |
| `mub` | Average premium paid by the government | `perc_en` | Percentage of energy import to total import |
| `lambdarow` | Share of government bills held by foreign sector | `yf` | Foreign income |
| `rho` | Reserve ratio |  |  |

In this notebook's adjusted run, the companion other-payments flows `opf`, `opb`
and `opcb` are also held to observed history. They are part of the unadjusted
STATIC exogenization set above and remove a very slow accounting block around
firms' profit distribution under the stricter notebook solver tolerance.

This notebook keeps the baseline unadjusted on purpose, so the charts compare the
model's simulated path against observed history rather than forcing the fitted values
onto the observed series.

cells.title: Adjusted in-sample run (1997-2021)
cells.description: STATIC tracking run matching the reference repository's adjusted in-sample prediction. The listed endogenous variables are held to observed Eurostat paths while the remaining equations and accounting identities solve.

cells.title: Fig. 2 Adjusted in-sample run vs observed (1997-2021)

charts.title: Fig. 2(g) Average yield on gov. securities
Fig. 2(h) Government debt to GDP ratio

cells.title: Out-of-sample prediction
cells.source:
Section 4 of `marcoverpas/Italy-SFC-Model` extends the adjusted in-sample fit into a 2022-2028 baseline with a single `SIMULATE(..., simType = "DYNAMIC")` call over the whole 1998-2028 range. The reference code pins the behavioural variables to observed data only through 2021 (`Exogenize = c(1998,1,2021,1)`) and then releases them, so the one run reproduces the adjusted historical path and continues forward, feeding back its own simulated lagged endogenous values from 2022 onward.

This notebook mirrors that with one continuous run (`out-of-sample-run`, 1997-2028) that uses windowed `exogenize` (`throughPeriod: 25`): each listed variable stays pinned to observed history for 1997-2021, then its equation is restored and solved for 2022-2028. The reference `ConstantAdjustment` list is represented as explicit additive externals (`adj_*`) that are zero in-sample and carry the R add-factor path over 2022-2028, keeping the forward run transparent.
cells.more:
The single segmented run works as follows:

1. Periods 1-25 (1997-2021) solve with the behavioural set held to observed data, so
   this window reproduces the adjusted in-sample path.
2. Periods 26-32 (2022-2028) release that set and solve it dynamically, seeded from the
   2021 state. Exogenous inputs (e.g. `dum`, the `opf`/`oph` other-payments closures)
   are held at their last observed value through 2028.
3. The `adj_*` series externals add the R `constantAdjList` corrections over 2022-2028:

| Adjustment external | R target | 2022-2028 values |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `adj_Lp` | GDP deflator correction | `[-0.017, -0.01, 0.006, 0.018, 0.03, 0.04, 0.045]` |
| `adj_LimR` | Real imports correction | `[0.156, 0.125, 0.111, 0.111, 0.111, 0.111, 0.111]` |
| `adj_LxR` | Real exports correction | `[0.075, 0.063, 0.065, 0.065, 0.065, 0.065, 0.065]` |
| `adj_LconsR` | Real consumption correction | `[0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.05, 0.05, 0.045, 0.045]` |
| `adj_deltak` | Depreciation correction | `[0.0126, 0.0069, 0.0011, -0.0016, -0.0033, -0.0033, -0.0033]` |
| `adj_Lp_en` | Energy-price shock | `[0.38, 0.055, 0.06, 0, 0, 0, 0]` |
| `adj_rstar` | Policy-rate path around `r0` | `[-0.01858333, -0.00298333, 0.01941667, 0.01141667, 0.00641667, 0.00641667, 0.00641667]` |
| `adj_mub` | Bills-premium correction | `[-0.004, -0.02, -0.014, -0.01, -0.008, -0.008, -0.008]` |
| `adj_gov` | Government spending correction | `[0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]` |

cells.title: Out-of-sample run (1997-2028)
cells.description: Single continuous DYNAMIC run. The behavioural variables stay pinned to observed data through 2021 (windowed exogenize) so 1997-2021 reproduces the adjusted in-sample path, then they are released and solved dynamically for 2022-2028. The R out-of-sample add-factor path enters through the explicit adj_* series externals.

cells.title: Fig. 3 Continuous in-sample + out-of-sample dynamic run (1997-2028)

charts.title: Fig. 3(g) Average yield on gov. securities
Fig. 3(h) Government debt to GDP ratio

cells.title: Additional experiments
cells.source: Section 6 of `marcoverpas/Italy-SFC-Model` compares the out-of-sample baseline with three alternative experiments over 2022-2028. This notebook shows each alternative as a full 1997-2028 segmented dynamic run, just like `out-of-sample-run`: periods 1-25 (1997-2021) stay pinned to observed history, then periods 26-32 (2022-2028) solve forward with the experiment-specific add-factor path.
cells.more:
- Scenario 1 makes the energy-led price shock more persistent, keeping CPI inflation around 5% in the forecast window.
- Scenario 2 combines that persistent inflation shock with an ECB policy response that maintains the policy rate at 4.5% over 2024-2026, plus the reference consumption-volume adjustment.
- Scenario 3 applies fiscal-austerity cuts designed to bring the deficit-to-GDP ratio toward the Maastricht 3% threshold from 2024 onward.

cells.title: Alternative scenario 1 run (1997-2028)
cells.description: Full segmented DYNAMIC run with the persistent energy-price shock over 2022-2028 replacing the baseline adj_Lp_en path.

cells.title: Alternative scenario 2 run (1997-2028)
cells.description: Full segmented DYNAMIC run with persistent inflation, the ECB policy response, and the consumption-volume adjustment over 2022-2028.

cells.title: Alternative scenario 3 run (1997-2028)
cells.description: Full segmented DYNAMIC run with government-spending add-factor cuts over 2022-2028.

charts.title: Scenario 2: CPI inflation and policy rate
